response:
The area of the regular hexagonal community center is determined by dividing the shape into six equilateral triangles, each with a side length of thirty meters, and applying the trigonometric formula for the area of a triangle involving the sine of sixty degrees. Since the area of one such triangle is calculated as one-half times the side squared times the sine of the interior angle, multiplying this result by six yields a total area of two thousand seven hundred fifty root three square meters, which represents the precise floor space available for the new facility.


The circular garden inscribed within the hexagon has a radius equal to the apothem of the polygon, which is found by using the tangent of thirty degrees or by recognizing the thirty-sixy-niney degree right triangle formed by the center, a vertex, and the midpoint of a side. This geometric relationship reveals that the radius is fifteen root three meters, and applying the standard formula for the area of a circle using this radius results in an area of six hundred seventy-five root three square meters for the central garden.
